metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Operationalize crash-resume AC sub-claim (b) with a concrete proving command — e.g., assert `git rev-list --count HEAD` after re-run equals (initial committed chunks × commits-per-chunk) + (remaining chunks × commits-per-chunk), or spy on `source_id` lookup calls to assert already-committed event ids are not re-staged.
findings.finding: Crash-resume AC sub-claim (b) — 'the source_id re-scan skips already-committed events' — has no concrete proving command; an executor cannot determine what observable evidence would satisfy this sub-claim versus sub-claim (a) alone.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — Crash-resume test bullet, sub-claim (b)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the crash-resume AC: 'assert git rev-list --count HEAD after re-run == total_expected_commits (i.e. the pre-crash committed chunk count plus the newly committed remaining chunks), confirming no events from already-committed chunks were re-written.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Revise the crash-resume scenario to crash during the SECOND chunk (after the first chunk of ~256 events is successfully committed): assert the re-run skips the first chunk's already-committed events (source_id re-scan returns ~256 entries) and re-emits only the second chunk's events — this meaningfully exercises the source_id deduplication path that B2 guarantees.
findings.finding: The crash-resume test scenario crashes 'before a chunk's git commit', meaning zero events are committed to the store. Yet the plan's post-re-run assertion (b) says 'the source_id re-scan skips already-committed events'. With zero committed events, the re-scan finds nothing to skip and the re-run imports everything from scratch — assertion (b) is vacuous and does not exercise the source_id deduplication path that B2 specifies. The scenario as written cannot distinguish correct deduplication from the absence of any prior state.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — Crash-resume test, assertion (b); Scope section: 'assert (a) rebar.list_tickets returns every source ticket exactly once … and (b) the source_id re-scan skips already-committed events'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in AC4 that `tests/unit/test_store_batch_write.py` is a file B1 must create (not a pre-existing regression guard), and gate AC4's execution on B1 being merged.
findings.finding: `tests/unit/test_store_batch_write.py` is named in AC4 as a 'verified path' (green regression suite), but it is a NEW file that B1 must create — it does not currently exist. The B1 manifest explicitly records it as `dep absent tests/unit/test_store_batch_write.py`.
findings.suggested_fix: Annotate AC4 as: '`tests/unit/test_store_batch_write.py` (created by B1 — verify B1 is merged before running this suite)'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in AC-4 that `tests/unit/test_store_batch_write.py` is created by B1 (not pre-existing) and add a precondition note that this AC line is only verifiable after B1 is merged.
findings.finding: `tests/unit/test_store_batch_write.py` is listed in AC-4 as a named regression suite that must be green, but this file does not currently exist — it is a B1 creation artifact (B1 ticket 890d is `in_progress`, and the file is marked `dep absent` in B1's signed manifest). B4 does not note that AC-4's regression gate is only satisfiable after B1 is merged and that file is written.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a parenthetical to AC-4: `tests/unit/test_store_batch_write.py` *(created by B1 — verify B1 is merged before running this suite)*.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit `git rev-list --count HEAD` unchanged assertion to the malformed-event-mid-chunk AC to prove no commit was recorded, not only that the index is clean.
findings.finding: The malformed-event-mid-chunk AC asserts 'no partial commit' but only names `git diff --cached --name-only` (index-clean check) as the proving command; it omits an explicit `git rev-list --count HEAD` unchanged assertion to confirm no partial commit was actually recorded.
findings.suggested_fix: Amend the AC to: 'capture rev-count before injecting the malformed event; after the failed batch, assert both `git diff --cached --name-only` is empty AND `git rev-list --count HEAD` equals the pre-test count.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Tighten the crash-resume monkeypatch specification: instead of replacing all of `event_append.subprocess.run`, specify a predicate (e.g. intercept only when `args[3] == 'commit'` or use a call-counting stub that raises only when the command list contains `'commit'`) so the fault is injected precisely after `git add` succeeds and before `git commit` commits, matching the 'after staging' intent.
findings.finding: The crash-resume test monkeypatches 'event_append.subprocess.run' to raise 'after staging', but batch_stage_and_commit makes multiple subprocess.run calls (git add, git commit, and within _recover_from_unmerged: git diff, git rm, git checkout, git add, git commit); additionally _seam.author() calls subprocess.run(['git','config','user.name']) before the batch primitive is reached. A naive replacement of subprocess.run raises on the first call (git add or git config), not the git commit, so the 'after staging' invariant is broken — the test would assert index-clean for the wrong reason and never verify the commit-level rollback.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — Crash-resume test; also Scope section: 'monkeypatch event_append.subprocess.run so the FIRST chunk's git commit raises after staging'
findings.suggested_fix: In the AC and Scope, specify the monkeypatch as: `original = subprocess.run; def _crashing_run(cmd, **kw): if 'commit' in cmd: raise RuntimeError('simulated crash'); return original(cmd, **kw); monkeypatch.setattr('rebar._store.event_append.subprocess.run', _crashing_run)` — this intercepts only the git commit call, leaving git add and git config intact.
Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether B1's `batch_stage_and_commit` exposes a named `_validate_event` helper (or equivalent callable), and if not, restate the malformed-event-mid-chunk AC to say 'passes an event dict with an invalid `event_type` or missing `uuid` directly to `batch_stage_and_commit`' rather than referencing `_validate_event` by name.
findings.finding: The plan's Scope and AC reference `_validate_event` as a named callable on the batch primitive ('one event that fails `_validate_event`'), but B1's AC and the current `event_append.py` expose no such named function — validation is currently inline in `stage_and_commit`. If B1 implements batch validation the same way (inline, not as a named helper), the malformed-event-mid-chunk test has no stable monkeypatch/call target and may require reformulating the test (e.g., passing a structurally invalid event dict directly rather than patching a named validator).
findings.location: Scope (second bullet) and AC line 2: 'fails the primitive's validation (invalid `event_type` / missing `uuid`)' / 'fails `_validate_event`'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'fails `_validate_event`' in the Scope and AC with 'passes an event dict with an invalid `event_type` or missing `uuid` field directly to `batch_stage_and_commit`', which is the actual observable trigger regardless of whether B1 names the validator.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the implicit `_validate_event` function reference in the malformed-event AC with the concrete observable: 'pass a `(ticket_id, event_dict)` where `event_dict` has an invalid `event_type` or missing `uuid` directly to `batch_stage_and_commit`' — so the test is specified in terms of the batch primitive's public interface, not an internal helper name.
findings.finding: The malformed-event AC refers to 'an event dict that fails the primitive's validation' by the implicit function name `_validate_event`, but no such named function exists in the current codebase (the validation is done inline in `stage_and_commit`), and B1's AC never commits to extracting a `_validate_event` helper. If B1 keeps validation inline, the AC phrase 'fails `_validate_event`' is unverified as a callable symbol — implementors may waste time searching for or creating a non-existent hook instead of simply passing a bad event dict.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — Malformed-event-mid-chunk test bullet: 'fails the primitive's validation (invalid `event_type` / missing `uuid`)'
findings.suggested_fix: Rewrite the AC bullet as: 'Malformed-event-mid-chunk test: call `batch_stage_and_commit(tracker, [(tid, bad_event)])` where `bad_event` carries an invalid `event_type` or missing `uuid`; assert the whole chunk rolls back — index clean (`git diff --cached --name-only` empty), no partial commit, no phantom staged blob.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the reference to `_validate_event` in AC2 with the actual mechanism: either 'passes an event dict with an invalid `event_type` or missing `uuid` to `batch_stage_and_commit`' (invoking inline validation), or update once B1's batch validation interface is known.
findings.finding: The malformed-event AC (AC2) references 'fails `_validate_event`' as the mechanism for triggering chunk rollback, but no function named `_validate_event` exists anywhere in the codebase. Validation in the current `stage_and_commit` is inline and unnamed.
findings.location: AC2: 'a chunk containing an event dict that fails the primitive's validation (invalid `event_type` / missing `uuid`) rolls the whole chunk back'
findings.suggested_fix: AC2: 'a chunk containing an event dict with `event_type="BOGUS"` or missing `uuid` key passed to `batch_stage_and_commit` raises `StoreError` and rolls the whole chunk back…'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the hallucinated `_validate_event` symbol reference with the actual validation mechanism B1 exposes (e.g. the condition/exception that `batch_stage_and_commit` raises for an invalid event), confirmed against B1's delivered code before implementing AC-2.
findings.finding: The malformed-event AC and the Scope section name `_validate_event` as the validation function whose failure triggers whole-chunk rollback, but this symbol does not appear anywhere in the current codebase (`src/rebar/_store/event_append.py` or elsewhere) and is not named in B1's acceptance criteria. B1's AC says 'validation' generically. This may be a hallucinated symbol name that will not match what B1 actually delivers.
findings.location: Scope (malformed-event-mid-chunk test) and AC-2: 'fails the primitive's validation (invalid `event_type` / missing `uuid`)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the non-existent `_validate_event` reference in Scope and AC-2 with the actual validation mechanism B1 implements (e.g. 'an event dict with `event_type` not in `EVENT_TYPES` or missing `uuid`/`timestamp` raises `StoreError` and triggers rollback'), or confirm that B1 will expose a named `_validate_event` helper and update B1's AC accordingly.
findings.finding: `_validate_event` is referenced in Scope and AC-2 as the named callable whose failure triggers whole-chunk rollback, but this function does not exist anywhere in `src/rebar/_store/event_append.py` or the broader codebase. Validation in the current `stage_and_commit` is inline (checks `event_type not in EVENT_TYPES`, missing `timestamp`/`uuid`), and B1's AC never names a `_validate_event` function. An executor of AC-2 would have no callable to target or monkeypatch — the referenced decision-rule protocol is undefined.
findings.suggested_fix: In Scope and AC-2, replace '`_validate_event`' with the concrete inline check: 'an event dict whose `event_type` is not in `EVENT_TYPES` or that is missing `uuid`/`timestamp` (causing `StoreError` to be raised inside the batch primitive)'. If B1 intends to extract this into a named helper, add that to B1's scope explicitly.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the hardcoded `256` in the AC formula and test implementation with a reference to the named constant exported from B1 (e.g., `from rebar.store import BATCH_SIZE`) so the assertion tracks the primitive automatically.
findings.finding: The batch size `256` is hardcoded as a magic number in the AC formula (`2*ceil(3000/256)`) and the benchmark assertion. If the batch size in B1 (`batch_stage_and_commit`) is ever tuned or made configurable, the test will silently assert the wrong commit count without a compiler/import error, obscuring the regression.
findings.suggested_fix: In the test file, import the batch-size constant from B1 (e.g., `from rebar.store.batch import BATCH_SIZE`) and compute the expected delta as `2 * math.ceil(3000 / BATCH_SIZE)`. Update the AC wording to 'equals `2*ceil(3000/BATCH_SIZE)` where `BATCH_SIZE` is the constant defined in B1'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that B1 (890d) is merged and `batch_stage_and_commit` exists in `src/rebar/_store/event_append.py` before writing any B4 test that invokes or monkeypatches it.
findings.finding: `batch_stage_and_commit` does not exist in the current codebase. The plan's Scope, every AC, and the crash-resume/malformed-event tests all assert behavioral properties of this primitive, but B1 (890d) is still `in_progress` and the function is absent from `src/rebar/_store/event_append.py`.
findings.location: Scope section; AC1 (commit-count delta); AC2 (malformed-event rollback); AC3 (crash-resume)
findings.suggested_fix: Add an explicit precondition gate: at the top of `tests/unit/test_store_batch_write.py` (and the B4 test file), import `batch_stage_and_commit` and let an `ImportError` surface immediately rather than a silent wrong-path test.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm both B1 (890d, `batch_stage_and_commit`) and B2 (6386, `_batch_sink` in `_seam.py` + importer pass wrapping) are merged before executing any B4 benchmark or crash-resume test — add a runtime assertion or import guard to the B4 test file.
findings.finding: The `_batch_sink` contextvar and `batch_sink()` contextmanager (B2 deliverables in `src/rebar/_commands/_seam.py`) are asserted to exist as the mechanism by which the importer routes events into batch commits, but neither exists in the current codebase (B2 is `open`, not started).
findings.location: Scope section: 'run `import_tickets`, and assert the commit-count DELTA == `2*ceil(3000/256)` (=24)'; AC1 benchmark test
findings.suggested_fix: At the top of the B4 benchmark test: `from rebar._commands._seam import batch_sink` and `from rebar._store.event_append import batch_stage_and_commit` — an `ImportError` will surface cleanly rather than a misleading wrong-count assertion.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a Scope bullet (or a 'Regression guard' sub-section) that explicitly describes the rationale for the named test files in AC4, so the criterion is traceable to described work.
findings.finding: AC4 names five specific regression test files (`tests/unit/test_store_batch_write.py`, `tests/interfaces/store/test_reducer_single_source.py`, etc.) as a named verification step, but the Scope section contains no corresponding work item describing the selection, curation, or rationale for this specific file set. This makes AC4 an orphan criterion: it cannot be traced to any described deliverable or task in Scope/What.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC4 ('Named regression suites green')
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Scope: 'Regression guard: confirm that B4's new tests do not regress the existing batch-write unit tests, reducer/replay tests, union-merge convergence tests, and snapshot compaction tests by running the named suites in AC4.' This makes AC4 traceable to a described task.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify per-test repo isolation (e.g., each test case gets its own `tmp_path`-backed `init_repo` fixture) and define an explicit teardown/cleanup for the git index and working tree so that a mid-test failure in the crash-resume case cannot contaminate the benchmark or malformed-event tests.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify test-repo isolation — whether each of the three test cases (benchmark, malformed-event, crash-resume) receives a fresh `init_repo` fixture or shares one — and does not define a teardown/cleanup path for the git index and commit state that the crash-resume test intentionally corrupts. If the crash-resume assertion fails mid-test, the dirty index and/or partial commits persist and can contaminate the benchmark or malformed-event tests that rely on a clean commit-count delta and an empty `--cached` index.
findings.location: Scope / Acceptance Criteria — Crash-resume test and Benchmark test
findings.suggested_fix: Use a pytest `tmp_path`-scoped fixture for each test function (function scope, not session/module scope) so each test gets a pristine repo. Add a `finally` block or `autouse` fixture that runs `git checkout -- .` and `git reset HEAD` on teardown to ensure the index is clean even if the crash-resume assertion raises.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
